explanation.text: The narrator spends weeks quietly familiarizing Ranger with her voice and movement, then uses carrots as gentle rewards until he accepts people. His growing trust supports the claim that patience and kindness can help a wild horse overcome fear of humans.
Ranger's pounding hooves kick up dust while his tail streams behind him like a banner as he gallops. These energetic images show that he is moving at great speed.

Ranger suddenly gallops into view outside the supposedly safe corral, prompting the narrator to demand how he escaped. This event shows the central conflict beginning: she must manage a powerful horse that can jump the fence at will.

Instead of rushing Ranger, the narrator spends weeks letting him adjust to her voice and movements while she quietly proves she is not a threat. This sustained patience best supports the story's theme that trust develops through gentle, consistent effort.
Ranger refuses unfamiliar treats and even bucks away at the sound of a carrot breaking because wild mustangs distrust new flavors. This behavior indicates that he is cautious about unfamiliar food.

Ranger watches Ghost eat a carrot, edges closer to investigate, and hesitates before taking his own bite. His decision to approach and taste it shows that curiosity is beginning to overcome his distrust.

The narrator shares her private expectations, panic, guesses about Ranger's motives, and interpretations of his expressions throughout the story. These thoughts reveal and develop her changing view of the horse from dangerous escape artist to trusting partner.

Hummingbirds must navigate precisely while repeatedly traveling among blossoms to feed. Being able to see both forward and sideways supports that movement because the birds can track nearby flowers and their path at the same time.

The section identifies the Americas as hummingbirds' range and lists habitats from jungles and Alaskan regions to coasts, deserts, city parks, and gardens. These geographic details mainly explain where the birds can be found.

The table directs readers to countryside nests, neighborhood flower beds and feeders, and parks in New York City. These varied locations support the statement that hummingbirds raise families in wilderness areas, city parks, and backyard gardens.

A hummingbird traveling quickly can still stop suddenly and land gracefully on a narrow perch. That combination of speed, control, and precision demonstrates that the bird is highly skilled at flying.

The article surveys where hummingbirds live, feed, migrate, and fly, so their range is an important broad fact. The statement that they live wherever flowers bloom in the western half of the world belongs in a summary, while the other choices are narrower examples.

The author first calls the restaurant kitchen mysterious, then contrasts his simple home stove, refrigerator, sink, and table with a bustling space full of gigantic woks and specialized equipment. This contrast shows the restaurant kitchen's unfamiliar, impressive quality.

The restaurant gives the children unfamiliar foods such as preserved plums, whose intense taste they learn to appreciate, along with pickled scallions and sugar cane. These examples show the author gradually enjoying strange snacks available through restaurant life.

Whenever the father tastes a candy he likes, he attempts to reproduce it for the restaurant, even developing his own rock candy. This willingness to invent a version of a product demonstrates the creativity useful to a restaurant owner.

The broad statement that the father would try anything is supported by his experiments with pastries and homemade candy, including an unsuccessful rock candy. It reveals his adventurous, inventive approach more fully than a single physical action does.

Gim Bok invents the playful warning that swallowing an apple seed will make a tree grow inside Shirley's stomach. This impossible, teasing tale shows that "spin stories" means create humorous stories.

The memories repeatedly center on eating restaurant foods: barbecued pork, cream-puff-like treats, experimental candy, preserved plums, scallions, and sugar cane. This evidence shows that the range of foods is the main way the author represents his childhood experience.

The author spent his first ten years around the restaurant, sometimes treated it as his actual home, and recalls its kitchen, food, family routines, and workers in rich detail. This extensive evidence most strongly supports his claim that the cafe was his home away from home.
